title: Error: chtimes /var/lib/containers/storage/volumes/my-volume-name/_data: permission denied
body:
**Description**

I have a Kubernetes deployment where I run the podman api (privileged) and an ubuntu container that uses podman (via podman-remote as user 1000) automatically via a Jenkins automations. Before starting my container, Jenkins creates a podman volume that mounts a cifs volume.

In the ubuntu container my-deployment as user 1000 via Jenkins:
```
podman-remote volume create --driver local --opt type=cifs --opt device=//some.ip.goes.here/A_FOLDER --opt o=username=guest,password='' my-volume-name
```

After the volume is created, Jenkins tries to start a new container that should have access to this volume, again in the ubuntu container my-deployment as user 1000

```
podman-remote run -dt --mount type=bind,source=/my_base_path/scripts/,destination=/scripts --mount type=volume,source=my-volume-name,destination=/mnt--net=private --uts=private my-image:latest
```

When trying to start the container, Jenkins gets the following error message:

```
 Error: chtimes /var/lib/containers/storage/volumes/my-volume-name/_data: permission denied
```

Rerunning the `run` command in Jenkins does not help.

If I `kubectl exec` manually into my ubuntu container an re-run the run command, the container starts (even though the user (verified via whoami and id) is the same in both cases.

**Steps to reproduce the issue:**

1. Create a container that can run podman-remote
2. Deploy to Kubernetes (see below)
3. Create a podman volume (either NFS or CIFS)
4. Run a new container, e.g. busybox from within the container created in 1. and try to mount the volume.

**Describe the results you received:**

```
 Error: chtimes /var/lib/containers/storage/volumes/my-volume-name/_data: permission denied
```

**Describe the results you expected:**

The volume should be mounted or at least the behaviour between the execution via Jenkins pipeline and kubectl exec should be consistent.

**Additional information you deem important (e.g. issue happens only occasionally):**

**Output of `podman version`:**

```
podman version 3.3.1
```

**Output of `podman info --debug`:**

```

```

```
apiVersion: v1

        runAsUser: 1000
      env:
        - name: CONTAINER_HOST
          value: "tcp://localhost:9999"
```

**EDIT**
Removed `ro` from mount as this was a copy and paste mistake.

comments.body: Permission denied is almost always SELinux or User Permissions. Looks like SELinux is disabled.

Hey,

Thank you for the quick response. The strange thing is that it does not work when executed via Jenkins. But if I leave the pod running and then kubectl exec into (I.e. The same pod Jenkins started), it then works. So if SElinux would be to blame, I would expect it to never work...

In fact `id -Z` yields:

```
13:29:40  id: --context (-Z) works only on an SELinux-enabled kernel
```

Best regards,
stiller-leser
What do you mean when you say that if you `kubectl exec` in, it works - are you running a container with the volume attached to it manually, and that works?
Also - the volume is mounted as read-only, but it seems Jenkins is trying to modify it (at the very least, to change the modification time?) - this seems expected? Read-only means read-only.

> Also - the volume is mounted as read-only, but it seems Jenkins is trying to modify it (at the very least, to change the modification time?) - this seems expected? Read-only means read-only.

Hey, yes this was in fact a copy and paste mistake. Sorry about this

>What do you mean when you say that if you kubectl exec in, it works - are you running a container with the volume attached to it manually, and that works?

I mean, if Jenkins starts the pod (it does not work), but when it leaves the pod running and I then `kubectl exec` from my local machine into the same pod it does work.

The ID you ran above was that in on the node or in the container.  id -Z inside of a container will lie to you about SELinux being enabled.

I believe the issue is either your kubectl exec is running the success  full process with a different user or SELinux label, and this is why it succeeds.
It was run inside the container, not the node. Is there anything more than `id` or anything that I could check? Unfortunately I am not that versed in determining such things.
Again, what do you do *after* you `kubectl exec`? Are you running a container that contains the volume?

cat /proc/self/attr/current 

if set, then you are on an SELinux system.
@stiller-leser Along with what @rhatdan mentioned could you also please paste output of `id` executed from `container` arg and the one you get after `kubectl exec`

Hi everybody, 

I tried creating a picture to hopefully explain this a little better:

![grafik](https://user-images.githubusercontent.com/2844279/140938217-bafb9235-e8f8-4256-8114-7f993145b9e5.png)

Here's what I tried to do in the meantime:

- Ran `cat /proc/self/attr/current`, which returned `docker-default (enforce)` in both cases
- Ran `id`, returned `uid=1000(my-user) gid=9999(my-group) groups=9999(my-group)` in both cases
- Add both users in both containers to a common group with the name `my-group` and gid `9999` and created the volume with `-o guid=9999`
  - Did not help
- Mount the volume with `rw`
  - Did not help
- Verify that I am using the same kubeconfig for Jenkins and my local machine
- Compared both containers started by podman-remote run. The only difference I could find was the the working one had a `mergeddir` entry more under the overlayfs.
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.

I might be missing something here.  But it seems you have podman running in rootless mode as UID 1000 within the container. Then you report the error as
 Error: chtimes /var/lib/containers/storage/volumes/my-volume-name/_data: permission denied
WHich should only happen if podman thought it was running in rootful mode, and is getting permission denied, because podman running as UID 1000 is attempting to modify a file owned by root.  Are my assumptions correct?
A friendly reminder that this issue had no activity for 30 days.
